gh-w11-f1.answer:
BankingDatabase/ — production banking schema with accounts, transactions, customers, and optimized queries.

Project structure:
• BankingDatabase/
  - pom.xml — spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, mysql-connector-j, flyway-core
  - src/main/resources/db/migration/ — Flyway scripts V1__create_schema.sql, V2__seed_data.sql
  - src/main/resources/application.properties — datasource, flyway enabled
  - src/main/java/com/academy/banking/
    - BankingApplication.java
    - model/Customer.java — id, name, email, phone, kycStatus
    - model/Account.java — id, accountNumber, accountType (SAVINGS/CURRENT), balance, customerId (@ManyToOne)
    - model/Transaction.java — id, fromAccountId, toAccountId, amount, type (DEBIT/CREDIT/TRANSFER), timestamp, status
    - repository/CustomerRepository, AccountRepository, TransactionRepository
    - service/TransferService.java — @Transactional transfer logic
    - controller/ (optional REST layer for testing)
  - sql/stored-procedures/transfer.sql — stored procedure for atomic transfer
  - sql/indexes.sql — indexes on account_number, customer_id, transaction timestamp

Schema (3NF normalized):
• customers — no duplicate customer data
• accounts — FK to customers
• transactions — FK to accounts, immutable audit trail

Key queries to implement:
• Transfer between accounts (debit + credit in one transaction)
• Account balance inquiry
• Transaction history by account with date range
• Daily transaction summary

Key concepts: ACID transactions, 3NF normalization, Flyway versioned migrations, indexes on FK and frequently queried columns.
gh-w11-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Banking systems demand atomic transfers — money can't disappear if one UPDATE fails. Flyway migrations are how every fintech team deploys schema changes safely.

Setup:
CREATE DATABASE banking_db;

Run:
cd BankingDatabase
mvn spring-boot:run

Flyway runs migrations automatically on startup.

Push to springboot-week-4-database:
git add BankingDatabase/
git commit -m "Add BankingDatabase — normalized schema with Flyway migrations"
git push
gh-w11-f1.code:
-- V1__create_schema.sql
CREATE TABLE customers (
    id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    phone VARCHAR(20),
    kyc_status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'PENDING',
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE accounts (
    id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    account_number VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    account_type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    balance DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0.00,
    customer_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(id),
    INDEX idx_customer_id (customer_id)
);

CREATE TABLE transactions (
    id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    from_account_id BIGINT,
    to_account_id BIGINT,
    amount DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL,
    transaction_type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'COMPLETED',
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    INDEX idx_from_account (from_account_id),
    INDEX idx_created_at (created_at)
);

gh-w11-f2.answer:
ECommerceDatabase/ — e-commerce database with products, orders, customers, and advanced SQL queries.

Project structure:
• ECommerceDatabase/
  - pom.xml — JPA, MySQL, validation
  - model/Customer.java, Product.java, Category.java, Order.java, OrderItem.java
  - Relationships: Customer 1→N Order, Order 1→N OrderItem, Product N→M Category (join table product_categories)
  - repository/ with JPQL join queries
  - service/OrderService.java — place order, calculate totals
  - sql/views/sales_report_view.sql — CREATE VIEW for monthly sales
  - sql/queries/complex-joins.sql — standalone SQL exercises

Schema:
• categories — id, name
• products — id, name, sku, price, stock
• product_categories — product_id, category_id (many-to-many)
• customers — id, name, email
• orders — id, customer_id, order_date, status, total_amount
• order_items — id, order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price

Queries to implement:
• Top 10 best-selling products (JOIN + GROUP BY + ORDER BY COUNT)
• Customer order history with line items
• Products with zero orders (LEFT JOIN ... WHERE order_id IS NULL)
• Monthly revenue report (view or query)
• Paginated product search via Spring Data Pageable

Key concepts: Many-to-many join table, composite indexes on (order_id, product_id), JPQL vs native SQL, database views for reporting.
gh-w11-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: E-commerce is the classic relational schema interview question — orders, line items, products, categories. Sales report views power dashboards without hitting live tables every time.

Run:
cd ECommerceDatabase
mvn spring-boot:run

Test JPQL:
GET /api/products/search?keyword=laptop&page=0&size=10

Push:

gh-w11-f3.answer:
HospitalDatabase/ — hospital database with patients, doctors, appointments, and Hibernate entity mappings.

Project structure:
• HospitalDatabase/
  - pom.xml — JPA, MySQL, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa
  - model/Patient.java, Doctor.java, Appointment.java, Department.java
  - model/AuditableEntity.java — @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate (JPA Auditing)
  - repository/ with fetch join queries
  - dto/AppointmentSummaryDto.java — projection interface
  - service/AppointmentService.java
  - config/JpaAuditingConfig.java — @EnableJpaAuditing

Entity relationships:
• Department 1→N Doctor
• Patient 1→N Appointment
• Doctor 1→N Appointment
• Appointment @ManyToOne Patient, @ManyToOne Doctor

Lazy vs Eager strategy:
• Default LAZY on @ManyToOne — load on demand
• Use JOIN FETCH in queries that need related data
• DTO projections to avoid N+1: SELECT new AppointmentSummaryDto(a.id, p.name, d.name, a.dateTime)

Queries:
• Appointments by doctor on a date (fetch join patient)
• Patient history with all appointments
• Doctor schedule for a week

Bonus: @SQLDelete + @Where for soft delete on patients.

Key concepts: N+1 problem and fetch join fix, JPA Auditing for createdAt/updatedAt, DTO projections, cascade types (PERSIST not REMOVE on appointments).
gh-w11-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: N+1 is the most common Hibernate performance bug in production. Hospital appointment queries that load patient + doctor names need fetch joins or DTO projections — not lazy loading in a loop.

Run:
cd HospitalDatabase
mvn spring-boot:run

Enable SQL logging to spot N+1:
spring.jpa.show-sql=true
logging.level.org.hibernate.stat=DEBUG

Push:
git add HospitalDatabase/
git commit -m "Add HospitalDatabase — Hibernate mappings, fetch joins, auditing"
git push

gh-w11-f4.answer:
CRMDatabase/ — CRM database with contacts, leads, activities, dynamic query specifications, and MongoDB for activity logs.

Project structure:
• CRMDatabase/
  - pom.xml — JPA + MySQL + spring-boot-starter-data-mongodb + liquibase-core
  - Relational (MySQL): Contact, Lead entities
  - MongoDB: ActivityLog document — { leadId, type, description, metadata, createdAt }
  - repository/ContactRepository, LeadRepository
  - repository/LeadSpecification.java — JpaSpecificationExecutor for dynamic filters
  - repository/ActivityLogRepository extends MongoRepository
  - db/changelog/ — Liquibase XML or YAML migrations
  - service/LeadAnalyticsService.java — aggregation queries

Relational schema:
• contacts — id, name, email, company, phone, status
• leads — id, title, source, status, assigned_to, contact_id (nullable until converted)

Dynamic filters (Specification API):
• Filter leads by status AND assignedTo AND date range — built programmatically

MongoDB activity logs:
• High-write, flexible schema — activity types vary (CALL, EMAIL, MEETING, NOTE)
• Query by leadId, sort by createdAt desc

Analytics queries:
• Lead count by status (GROUP BY)
• Conversion rate: CONVERTED / total
• Activities per lead (MongoDB count)

Key concepts: Specification API for dynamic WHERE clauses, polyglot persistence (MySQL + MongoDB), Liquibase for relational migrations.
gh-w11-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: CRMs mix structured data (contacts, leads) with high-volume event logs (activities). MongoDB for activity streams + MySQL for relational integrity is a common hybrid architecture.

Run:
cd CRMDatabase
mvn spring-boot:run

Requires MySQL + MongoDB running.

Test dynamic filter:
GET /api/leads?status=NEW&assignedTo=john&fromDate=2026-01-01

Push:
git add CRMDatabase/
git commit -m "Add CRMDatabase — Specifications, MongoDB activity logs, Liquibase"
git push

gh-w11-f5.answer:
InventoryDatabase/ — inventory database with batch operations, HikariCP tuning, query optimization, and performance monitoring.

Project structure:
• InventoryDatabase/
  - pom.xml — JPA, MySQL, actuator
  - model/Warehouse.java, Product.java, StockMovement.java, Supplier.java
  - repository/ProductRepository — batch insert support
  - service/InventoryBatchService.java — @Transactional batch updates
  - sql/stored-procedures/low_stock_alert.sql
  - sql/backup/backup-restore.md — mysqldump instructions
  - config/HikariConfig notes in application.properties

Schema:
• warehouses — id, name, location
• products — id, sku, name, reorder_level
• stock — product_id, warehouse_id, quantity (composite PK)
• stock_movements — id, product_id, warehouse_id, quantity_change, reason, timestamp
• suppliers — id, name, contact

Performance features:
• Batch insert 1000 stock records — spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50
• HikariCP: maximum-pool-size, minimum-idle, connection-timeout
• EXPLAIN on slow queries — document before/after index
• Actuator /actuator/metrics for connection pool stats

Queries:
• Current stock by warehouse (JOIN stock + products)
• Stock movement history
• Low stock alert (quantity <= reorder_level)

Key concepts: JDBC batching, connection pooling, EXPLAIN plan analysis, composite indexes on (product_id, warehouse_id).
gh-w11-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Inventory systems process thousands of stock updates nightly. Batch inserts and pool tuning prevent connection exhaustion — the kind of issue that takes down warehouses on Black Friday.

Run:
cd InventoryDatabase
mvn spring-boot:run

HikariCP settings (application.properties):
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=10
spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=5
spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=30000
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.order_inserts=true

Push:
git add InventoryDatabase/
git commit -m "Add InventoryDatabase — batch ops, HikariCP, query optimization"
git push

gh-w11-f6.answer:
sql/advanced-queries.sql — standalone SQL file with advanced queries covering CTEs, window functions, subqueries, and aggregations across all Week 11 domains.

Include at least 10 queries:
1. Banking — CTE for running balance per account over time.
2. Banking — ROW_NUMBER() to rank customers by total transaction volume.
3. E-Commerce — RANK() top products by revenue with category.
4. E-Commerce — recursive CTE for category hierarchy (if applicable).
5. Hospital — COUNT appointments per doctor per month (GROUP BY + DATE_FORMAT).
6. Hospital — patients with no appointments (NOT EXISTS subquery).
7. CRM — lead conversion funnel (COUNT by status, CASE for stages).
8. CRM — leads with activity count (subquery or JOIN).
9. Inventory — moving average stock level (window function AVG() OVER).
10. Cross-domain — UNION ALL combining transaction counts from multiple systems (conceptual).

Each query must have:
• Comment block explaining purpose
• Expected columns listed
• Sample expected output as comment

Key concepts: CTE (WITH clause) for readable complex queries, window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD), correlated subqueries, HAVING for filtered aggregates.
gh-w11-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Analytics and reporting teams write SQL like this daily — not simple SELECT *. Window functions replace self-joins for ranking and running totals. Interviewers love CTE questions.

Run in MySQL Workbench or CLI:
mysql -u root -p banking_db < sql/advanced-queries.sql

Or execute queries one at a time against seeded databases.

Push:
git add sql/advanced-queries.sql
git commit -m "Add advanced SQL — CTEs, window functions, analytics"
git push
gh-w11-f6.code:
-- sql/advanced-queries.sql

-- 1. Running balance per account (Banking)
-- Purpose: Show cumulative balance after each transaction
WITH txn_ordered AS (
    SELECT account_id, amount, transaction_type, created_at,
           CASE WHEN transaction_type = 'CREDIT' THEN amount ELSE -amount END AS signed_amount
    FROM transactions
    WHERE status = 'COMPLETED'
)
SELECT account_id, created_at, signed_amount,
       SUM(signed_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY created_at
           ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_balance
FROM txn_ordered;

-- 2. Top 5 customers by transaction volume (Banking)
SELECT c.name, COUNT(t.id) AS txn_count, SUM(t.amount) AS total_volume,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(t.amount) DESC) AS volume_rank
FROM customers c
JOIN accounts a ON a.customer_id = c.id
JOIN transactions t ON t.from_account_id = a.id OR t.to_account_id = a.id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
ORDER BY total_volume DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- 3. Monthly appointment count per doctor (Hospital)
SELECT d.name AS doctor_name,
       DATE_FORMAT(a.appointment_date_time, '%Y-%m') AS month,
       COUNT(*) AS appointment_count
FROM doctors d
JOIN appointments a ON a.doctor_id = d.id
GROUP BY d.id, d.name, DATE_FORMAT(a.appointment_date_time, '%Y-%m')
ORDER BY month DESC, appointment_count DESC;

-- 4. Lead conversion funnel (CRM)
SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS lead_count,
       ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 2) AS percentage
FROM leads
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY FIELD(status, 'NEW', 'CONTACTED', 'QUALIFIED', 'CONVERTED', 'LOST');

gh-w11-f7.answer:
migrations/ — database migration scripts folder using Flyway and/or Liquibase for version-controlled schema evolution.

Folder structure:
• migrations/
  - flyway/
    - banking/V1__create_customers.sql
    - banking/V2__create_accounts.sql
    - banking/V3__create_transactions.sql
    - banking/V4__add_audit_columns.sql
    - ecommerce/V1__create_schema.sql
    - hospital/V1__create_schema.sql
  - liquibase/
    - crm/db.changelog-master.xml
    - crm/changes/001-create-contacts.sql
    - crm/changes/002-create-leads.sql
    - crm/changes/003-add-indexes.sql
  - README.md — how to run Flyway vs Liquibase, naming conventions

Flyway naming: V{version}__{description}.sql — runs in order, tracks in flyway_schema_history.
Liquibase: changelog master references individual changeSets with id and author.

Include at least one ALTER migration (add column), one index migration, and one seed data migration.

Key concepts: Migrations are immutable — never edit applied scripts, add new version instead. Flyway uses SQL; Liquibase supports rollback. Both integrate with Spring Boot auto-run on startup.
gh-w11-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: "Works on my machine" database drift kills teams. Migrations in Git mean dev, staging, and production schemas stay identical — applied automatically in CI/CD.

Flyway (Spring Boot):
spring.flyway.enabled=true
spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration

Liquibase:
spring.liquibase.change-log=classpath:db/changelog/db.changelog-master.xml

Run:
mvn flyway:migrate

Push:
git add migrations/
git commit -m "Add Flyway and Liquibase migration scripts"
git push

Rule: If V3 is already deployed to production, create V4 for changes — never modify V3.
gh-w11-f7.code:
-- migrations/flyway/banking/V4__add_audit_columns.sql
ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMN updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;
ALTER TABLE accounts ADD COLUMN updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;

<!-- migrations/liquibase/crm/db.changelog-master.xml -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<databaseChangeLog
    xmlns="http://www.liquibase.org/xml/ns/dbchangelog"
    xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.liquibase.org/xml/ns/dbchangelog
        http://www.liquibase.org/xml/ns/dbchangelog/dbchangelog-4.20.xsd">
    <include file="changes/001-create-contacts.sql" relativeToChangelogFile="true"/>
    <include file="changes/002-create-leads.sql" relativeToChangelogFile="true"/>
    <include file="changes/003-add-indexes.sql" relativeToChangelogFile="true"/>
</databaseChangeLog>

gh-w11-f8.answer:
mongodb/ — MongoDB scripts, document schemas, and Spring Data MongoDB examples for NoSQL data in Week 11 projects.

Folder structure:
• mongodb/
  - schemas/activity-log.json — sample document structure
  - schemas/product-catalog.json — flexible product document (nested variants)
  - scripts/seed-activity-logs.js — insert sample activity logs for CRM
  - scripts/aggregation-pipeline.js — analytics aggregations
  - spring-data/ActivityLog.java — @Document entity
  - spring-data/ActivityLogRepository.java — MongoRepository
  - README.md — when to use MongoDB vs MySQL in this curriculum

Sample activity log document:
{ leadId: 101, type: "CALL", description: "Follow-up call", metadata: { duration: 15, outcome: "interested" }, createdAt: ISODate("2026-06-20T10:00:00Z") }

Aggregation pipelines to include:
1. Count activities by type per leadId.
2. Most active leads in last 30 days.
3. Activity timeline for a specific lead (match + sort).

Spring Data MongoDB config:
spring.data.mongodb.uri=mongodb://localhost:27017/crm_logs

Key concepts: Document model vs relational tables, embedded vs referenced documents, aggregation pipeline ($match, $group, $sort, $lookup), eventual consistency vs ACID.
gh-w11-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Logs, events, and catalogs with varying fields fit MongoDB better than rigid SQL tables. Uber, eBay, and Forbes use MongoDB for high-write event streams alongside MySQL for core data.

Start MongoDB:
mongod --dbpath /data/db

Seed data:
mongosh crm_logs < mongodb/scripts/seed-activity-logs.js

Run aggregation in mongosh:
load('mongodb/scripts/aggregation-pipeline.js')

Push:

git commit -m "Add MongoDB schemas, seed scripts, and aggregations"
git push

gh-w11-f9.answer:
ER-Diagrams/ — Entity-Relationship diagrams for all five Week 11 database domains.

Folder structure:
• ER-Diagrams/
  - banking-er.png (or .drawio) — customers, accounts, transactions
  - ecommerce-er.png — products, categories, orders, order_items, customers
  - hospital-er.png — patients, doctors, departments, appointments
  - crm-er.png — contacts, leads, activities (note MongoDB for activity logs)
  - inventory-er.png — warehouses, products, stock, stock_movements, suppliers
  - README.md — how to read ER diagrams, cardinality notation

Each diagram must show:
• Entities (rectangles) with key attributes
• Primary keys (underlined or marked PK)
• Foreign keys and relationship lines
• Cardinality — 1:1, 1:N, M:N (crow's foot notation)
• Optional: mermaid source (.md) for GitHub rendering

Tools: draw.io (diagrams.net), dbdiagram.io, MySQL Workbench Reverse Engineer, or Mermaid in Markdown.

Key concepts: ER diagram is the blueprint before writing SQL. Cardinality (one customer has many accounts) drives FK design. Normalization decisions visible in diagram.
gh-w11-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: Architects review ER diagrams in design reviews before any code is written. Your portfolio ER diagrams prove you design data models, not just copy JPA tutorials.

Mermaid renders on GitHub — include .md files:

Push:
git add ER-Diagrams/
git commit -m "Add ER diagrams for all Week 11 database domains"
git push

Tip: Keep diagrams in sync with migrations — if you add a table in V5 migration, update the diagram.
gh-w11-f9.code:
# ER-Diagrams/banking-er.md

## Banking System ER Diagram

```mermaid
erDiagram
    CUSTOMERS ||--o{ ACCOUNTS : owns
    ACCOUNTS ||--o{ TRANSACTIONS : sends
    ACCOUNTS ||--o{ TRANSACTIONS : receives

    CUSTOMERS {
        bigint id PK
        varchar name
        varchar email UK
        varchar kyc_status
        timestamp created_at
    }

    ACCOUNTS {
        bigint id PK
        varchar account_number UK
        varchar account_type
        decimal balance
        bigint customer_id FK
    }

    TRANSACTIONS {
        bigint id PK
        bigint from_account_id FK
        bigint to_account_id FK
        decimal amount
        varchar transaction_type
        timestamp created_at
    }
```

## Cardinality
- One CUSTOMER has many ACCOUNTS (1:N)
- One ACCOUNT participates in many TRANSACTIONS (1:N as sender or receiver)

gh-w11-f10.code:
# springboot-week-4-database

Week 11 — Database Engineering & Advanced SQL for Prathyu Academy.

## Projects
| Project | Database | Highlights |
|---------|----------|------------|
| BankingDatabase | MySQL | 3NF schema, Flyway, transfers |
| ECommerceDatabase | MySQL | Joins, views, JPQL |
| HospitalDatabase | MySQL | Hibernate, N+1 fixes, auditing |
| CRMDatabase | MySQL + MongoDB | Specifications, activity logs |
| InventoryDatabase | MySQL | Batch ops, HikariCP tuning |

## Supporting Files
- `sql/advanced-queries.sql` — CTEs, window functions
- `migrations/` — Flyway & Liquibase scripts
- `mongodb/` — document schemas & aggregations
- `ER-Diagrams/` — entity-relationship models

## Quick Start
```bash
CREATE DATABASE banking_db;
cd BankingDatabase
mvn spring-boot:run
```

## Key Concepts
- Normalization (3NF) vs denormalization trade-offs
- Indexes and EXPLAIN plan analysis
- N+1 problem and fetch join solutions
- Flyway/Liquibase versioned migrations
- MongoDB for high-write event logs

## What I Learned
I designed normalized schemas, wrote advanced SQL with window functions, optimized Hibernate queries, and managed schema changes with Flyway and Liquibase migrations.
